title: Suki and the Friendship Map
synopsis: When Suki moves to a new neighborhood, she feels lost and lonely until she discovers an old treasure map in her backyard. Along with her new friends Marcus, Elena, and Zara, she embarks on a hilarious adventure that leads them not to buried gold, but to something much more valuable - true friendship and the discovery that the best treasures are the ones we create together.

chapters.title: New House, New Worries
chapters.content:
Suki pressed her nose against the moving truck window and sighed. The new house looked different from their old one. It was painted yellow instead of blue, and it had a big oak tree in the front yard instead of Mom's rose garden.

"Come on, Suki-bean," Dad said, opening the truck door. "Let's go explore our new home!"

Suki dragged her feet as she walked up the front steps. Everything smelled different here. Even the air seemed strange.

"I miss our old house," she mumbled.

Mom knelt down and gave her a hug. "I know, sweetheart. But remember what we talked about? Sometimes the best adventures start with something new."

"But what if nobody here likes me?" Suki asked. "What if I don't make any friends?"

"You will," Mom said with a smile. "You're funny and kind and creative. Who wouldn't want to be your friend?"

Suki wasn't so sure. At her old school, she knew everyone. Here, she didn't know anybody. Tomorrow was Monday, and she would have to start at Roosevelt Elementary School. Her tummy felt all twisty just thinking about it.

"Hey," Dad said, pointing to the backyard. "Look at all that space! And is that a treehouse?"

Suki perked up a little. She did love treehouses. Maybe this new place wouldn't be completely terrible.

illustrations.description: A young Japanese-American girl with shoulder-length black hair stands in front of a cheerful yellow house with a large oak tree, looking uncertain but hopeful. Her parents, a Japanese woman and a Black man, stand beside her with encouraging smiles. Moving boxes are scattered on the lawn, and golden afternoon light filters through the tree branches.
illustrations.alt: Suki and her parents standing in front of their new yellow house

chapters.title: The First Day Blues
chapters.content:
Monday morning came too fast. Suki's new school was bigger than her old one, with long hallways that seemed to go on forever.

"Class, we have a new student today," said Mrs. Rodriguez, her new teacher. "This is Suki Chen-Williams. Suki, would you like to tell us something about yourself?"

Suki felt her cheeks get hot. Twenty-four pairs of eyes stared at her. "Um... I just moved here from Portland. I like... um... drawing?"

"That's wonderful!" Mrs. Rodriguez said. "We love artists here. You can sit next to Elena."

A girl with curly brown hair and a friendly smile waved at her. "Hi!" Elena whispered as Suki sat down. "I like your backpack. Is that a dragon?"

"Yeah," Suki whispered back. "I drew it myself."

"Cool! I wish I could draw. I'm better at making things explode."

"Explode?" Suki's eyes went wide.

"Not like, boom explode," Elena giggled. "Like science experiments. Last week I made a volcano that shot foam everywhere. My mom was not happy."

For the first time since moving, Suki smiled. Maybe Elena could be a friend.

At lunch, Elena introduced her to two other kids. "This is Marcus," she said, pointing to a tall boy with dark skin and glasses. "And this is Zara." Zara had light brown skin and wore her hair in two puffs with colorful beads.

"Want to sit with us?" Marcus asked. "Elena was just telling us about her latest kitchen disaster."

"It wasn't a disaster!" Elena protested. "The smoke alarm was just being dramatic."

Suki giggled. These kids seemed pretty fun.

illustrations.description: A diverse classroom scene with Suki standing nervously at the front while Mrs. Rodriguez, a kind Latina teacher, introduces her. Students of various backgrounds sit at desks, including Elena with curly brown hair waving enthusiastically. Warm classroom lighting creates a welcoming atmosphere with colorful artwork on the walls.
illustrations.alt: Suki being introduced to her new classroom

chapters.title: The Mysterious Discovery
chapters.content:
After school, Suki decided to explore the backyard. The treehouse was even cooler up close. It had a rope ladder and everything!

As she climbed up, something crinkled under her foot. She looked down and saw the corner of an old piece of paper sticking out from between two boards.

"What's this?" she muttered, pulling it free.

It was yellow and wrinkled, like it had been there for a long time. Strange symbols and drawings covered the paper, along with what looked like a path marked with X's.

"No way," Suki breathed. "Is this a treasure map?"

She studied it more carefully. There was a drawing that looked like her house, and another that might be the school. The path led from one to the other, with stops at different places marked with symbols.

"Suki!" Mom called from the house. "Dinner!"

"Coming!" Suki called back. She carefully folded the map and put it in her pocket. This was the most exciting thing that had happened since they moved.

During dinner, she could barely sit still. "Mom, Dad, who lived in our house before us?"

"The Hendersons," Dad said. "They had kids, I think. Why?"

"Just wondering," Suki said, trying to sound casual. But inside, her mind was racing. Maybe the Henderson kids had made this map. Maybe they had hidden treasure somewhere in the neighborhood!

That night, she spread the map on her bed and studied it by flashlight. Tomorrow, she decided, she would show it to Elena, Marcus, and Zara. Maybe they would want to help her solve the mystery.

illustrations.description: Suki sits in a rustic wooden treehouse at golden hour, holding an old yellowed treasure map with mysterious symbols and pathways drawn on it. Her eyes are wide with excitement and wonder. Dappled sunlight filters through oak leaves, creating magical patterns on the weathered wood.
illustrations.alt: Suki discovering the treasure map in the treehouse

chapters.title: Recruiting the Crew
chapters.content:
The next day at lunch, Suki could hardly contain her excitement. "Guys," she whispered, "you have to see this."

She unfolded the map on the lunch table. Elena, Marcus, and Zara leaned in close.

"Whoa," Marcus said, pushing his glasses up his nose. "Where did you find this?"

"In the treehouse in my backyard. Look - I think these drawings are places around here."

"This totally looks like the school," Zara said, pointing. "And this might be the library."

"Or the park," Elena added. "Guys, this could be a real treasure map!"

"Probably just someone's art project," Marcus said. But Suki noticed he was studying the map very carefully.

"There's only one way to find out," Suki said. "We have to follow it."

"Yes!" Elena bounced in her seat. "I love adventures! When do we start?"

"Saturday morning?" Suki suggested. "We could meet at my house."

"I'm in," Zara said. "But what if we actually find treasure? How do we split it?"

"Four ways, even," Suki said. "We're all in this together."

Marcus finally smiled. "Okay, I'm in too. But I still think it's probably just a game someone made up."

"Even if it is," Elena said, "it'll be fun! Plus, I can bring snacks. My mom just bought those crackers that look like fish."

"Perfect," Suki said. For the first time since moving, she felt truly excited about something. She had friends, and they were going on an adventure together.

illustrations.description: Four diverse children huddle around a lunch table, examining the treasure map with excitement. Suki points to symbols while Elena bounces with enthusiasm, Marcus adjusts his glasses thoughtfully, and Zara traces paths with her finger. The school cafeteria bustles in the background with warm, natural lighting.
illustrations.alt: The four friends studying the treasure map at lunch

chapters.title: The Adventure Begins
chapters.content:
Saturday morning was perfect for treasure hunting. The sun was shining, and Suki had laid out all their supplies: the map, four water bottles, Elena's fish crackers, and a notebook for clues.

"Okay, crew," Suki said when everyone arrived. "Ready for our first real treasure hunt?"

"Ready!" Elena said, doing a little hop.

"I brought my mom's magnifying glass," Zara said, pulling it out of her backpack. "In case we need to examine clues."

"And I brought my compass," Marcus added. "You know, for navigation."

Suki grinned. "Perfect. According to the map, our first stop is... that way." She pointed toward the school.

They set off down the sidewalk, following the path marked on the map. The first symbol looked like a swing set, which had to be the playground behind the school.

"There!" Zara pointed when they arrived. "Now what?"

Suki studied the map. "It shows the swing set, and then... a circle with dots around it. What could that be?"

"Maybe a flower?" Elena suggested. "Or a clock?"

Marcus looked around the playground. "Wait - what about that?" He pointed to a round sign covered with holes. It was the playground's "Do Not Climb" sign, and someone had poked holes all around the edge.

"The circle with dots!" Suki said excitedly. "But now what?"

Zara used her magnifying glass to examine the sign closely. "Hey, there's something wedged behind here."

She pulled out a small piece of paper with another symbol drawn on it. "It's a clue!"

Elena jumped up and down. "This is the best treasure hunt ever!"

illustrations.description: The four friends stand at a colorful playground, with Zara using a magnifying glass to examine a circular sign while the others watch eagerly. Marcus holds a compass, Elena bounces with excitement, and Suki consults the treasure map. Morning sunlight creates long shadows across the playground equipment.
illustrations.alt: The friends discovering their first clue at the playground

chapters.title: Following the Clues
chapters.content:
The second clue led them to the library. The symbol on the paper looked like a book with a star on it.

"There are thousands of books in there," Marcus said, looking overwhelmed. "How do we find the right one?"

"Let's think," Suki said. "The star might mean it's special somehow."

Inside the library, they wandered around looking for anything that might match their clue. Finally, Elena spotted something.

"Look!" she whispered loudly. The librarian shushed her. "Sorry," Elena whispered more quietly. "But look at that display!"

It was a display of books with gold star stickers on them - the "Staff Picks" collection. And there, tucked between two books, was another piece of paper.

This clue showed a tree with something hanging from it. "That's got to be my treehouse," Suki said.

"So we're going back to where we started?" Zara asked.

"Maybe," Marcus said, studying the drawing. "But look - this tree looks different. It's smaller, and the thing hanging from it isn't a treehouse."

"A swing!" Elena said. "Remember? There's that old tire swing in Maple Park!"

They hurried to the park. Sure enough, hanging from the big maple tree was an old tire swing, just like in the drawing.

"Now what?" Zara wondered.

Suki climbed up to check the tree branches while Marcus examined the tire. Elena and Zara searched the ground around the base of the tree.

"Found it!" Elena called. She had discovered a small metal box buried under some leaves. Inside was yet another clue - and this one was different.

"It's not a drawing," Suki said, puzzled. "It's words. 'Where friends gather when the day is done, look for the place where laughter runs.'"

They all looked at each other. "What could that mean?"

illustrations.description: Elena crouches by the base of a large maple tree, holding up a small metal box while the other three friends gather around her. The old tire swing hangs from a thick branch above them, and autumn leaves are scattered on the ground. Golden afternoon light filters through the maple canopy.
illustrations.alt: Elena finding the metal box with a clue under the maple tree

chapters.title: The Riddle Challenge
chapters.content:
The four friends sat on a bench in the park, staring at the riddle.

"'Where friends gather when the day is done,'" Marcus read again. "That could be anywhere."

"And what's 'the place where laughter runs'?" Zara wondered. "Laughter doesn't run. It's not like... legs."

Elena giggled. "Maybe it means a place where lots of people laugh?"

"Like a comedy club?" Suki asked. "But we're kids. We don't go to comedy clubs."

"Think about our neighborhood," Marcus said. "Where do friends gather after school or on weekends?"

"The ice cream truck!" Elena shouted, then clapped her hand over her mouth when people looked at them.

"But the ice cream truck moves around," Zara pointed out.

"What about the community center?" Suki suggested.

They thought about this. The community center did have after-school programs, and kids were always laughing and playing there.

"It's worth a try," Marcus said.

When they got to the community center, they looked around for anything that might match their clue. There were kids playing basketball, some teenagers playing video games, and a group of adults in an exercise class.

"I don't see anything special," Zara said, discouraged.

Then Suki noticed something. "Wait - look at the bulletin board."

There, pinned among all the community notices and class schedules, was a small piece of paper with a drawing on it. It showed four stick figures holding hands around what looked like a house.

"Is that us?" Elena asked, amazed.

"I think so," Suki said softly. "But which house?"

Marcus grinned. "There's only one house we all know - yours, Suki. The treasure must be back at your place."

"We've been running around in a big circle!" Zara laughed.

illustrations.description: The four friends stand in front of a community center bulletin board covered with colorful flyers and notices. Suki points to a small drawing of four stick figures holding hands around a house. The community center lobby bustles with activity in the background, filled with warm, welcoming light.
illustrations.alt: The friends discovering the final clue at the community center

chapters.title: Back to the Beginning
chapters.content:
They raced back to Suki's house, everyone talking excitedly about what the treasure might be.

"Maybe it's gold coins!" Elena said, running backward so she could face the others.

"Or diamonds," Zara added.

"Probably just more paper," Marcus said, but he was grinning too.

"Where should we look?" Suki asked when they reached her backyard. "The treehouse again?"

"The drawing showed four people around a house," Marcus pointed out. "Not in a tree."

They spread out around the yard, looking for anything that might be a hiding spot. Elena checked behind the garbage cans. Zara looked under the back porch steps. Marcus examined the fence.

Suki stood in the middle of the yard, thinking. The map had led them all around the neighborhood and back home. What was special about this spot?

"Hey," she said slowly. "What if the treasure isn't a thing? What if it's a place?"

"What do you mean?" Elena asked.

"Think about it. We started at my house - the new place where I didn't know anyone. Then we went to all these places around the neighborhood, working together, solving puzzles. And now we're back here, but now I'm not alone anymore. I have friends."

Zara's eyes lit up. "You think the treasure is friendship?"

"Maybe. But let's keep looking anyway," Suki said with a grin. "Just in case."

That's when Marcus called out from near the oak tree. "Guys! There's something carved in the bark here!"

They all ran over. Sure enough, there were letters carved into the tree trunk: "F.T.F."

"F.T.F.?" Elena said. "What's that supposed to mean?"

Below the letters, they found one more thing: a small wooden box buried at the base of the tree.

illustrations.description: Marcus kneels at the base of the large oak tree, pointing excitedly at letters carved in the bark while the other three friends run toward him across the sunny backyard. A small wooden box sits partially buried among the tree roots, and the yellow house glows warmly in the background.
illustrations.alt: Marcus discovering the final treasure at the base of the oak tree

chapters.title: The Real Treasure
chapters.content:
With shaking hands, Suki opened the wooden box. Inside, there wasn't gold or diamonds. Instead, there were four friendship bracelets made of colorful string, and a note.

Suki unfolded the note and read it out loud:

"'Dear Future Friends, If you found this, it means you worked together to solve our puzzle. We are the Henderson kids - Jake, Mia, Carlos, and Sam. We lived in the yellow house and made this treasure hunt for whoever moved in after us. The real treasure isn't in this box. It's the adventure you shared and the friendships you made along the way. F.T.F. means Friends Through Fun. We hope you have as much fun in this neighborhood as we did. P.S. - The treehouse needs a new rope ladder. Welcome home!'"

"That's so cool!" Elena said, wiping her eyes. "They made this whole thing just for us!"

"Well, not exactly us," Marcus said. "But for whoever came next."

"Same thing," Zara said. She picked up one of the friendship bracelets. "Can I have the purple one?"

"I want the green one!" Elena called.

Marcus took the blue one, and Suki chose the red one. They all put them on.

"You know what?" Suki said, looking at her three friends. "I was so worried about moving here and not making friends. But this whole adventure brought us together."

"And now we know all the cool spots in the neighborhood," Zara added.

"We should make our own treasure hunt," Elena suggested. "For the next kids who move here."

"That's a great idea," Marcus said. "But first, didn't someone mention something about that rope ladder?"

They all looked up at the treehouse and grinned.

"Come on," Suki said. "Let's go fix our new headquarters."

As they walked toward the treehouse, Suki realized something. She wasn't the new kid anymore. She was home.

illustrations.description: The four friends sit in a circle on the grass, each wearing colorful friendship bracelets and smiling at each other. The opened wooden box sits in the center with the note spread out beside it. The treehouse is visible in the oak tree above them, and golden late-afternoon light bathes everything in warmth and friendship.
illustrations.alt: The friends wearing their friendship bracelets around the opened treasure box

chapters.title: New Beginnings
chapters.content:
Two weeks later, Suki's treehouse had been transformed. With help from all four sets of parents, they had installed a new rope ladder, painted the walls bright colors, and even added some cushions and a small bookshelf.

"Perfect headquarters," Elena said, settling into the cushions with a comic book.

"For our next adventure," Zara added, working on a new treasure map of her own.

Marcus was reading a book about codes and ciphers. "I think we should make our treasure hunt even trickier than the Henderson kids' one."

"But not too tricky," Suki said. She was drawing in her sketchbook - a picture of the four of them on their treasure hunt adventure. "We want the next kids to be able to solve it."

"Speaking of next kids," Elena said, "did you hear? The Johnsons are moving, and a family with twins is moving into their house."

"Twins!" Zara said excitedly. "That means two new friends instead of one."

"Maybe," Marcus said. "We should finish our treasure hunt soon, just in case."

Suki looked around at her friends - Elena with her wild curly hair full of leaves from climbing up to the treehouse, Zara with her beaded hair clips catching the sunlight, and Marcus with his glasses slightly crooked from all their adventures. A month ago, she didn't know any of them. Now she couldn't imagine life without them.

"You know what the best part of the Henderson treasure hunt was?" she asked.

"Finding the bracelets?" Elena guessed.

"Solving all those puzzles?" Marcus suggested.

"Learning about the neighborhood?" Zara tried.

"All of that," Suki said. "But mostly, it was doing it together. The treasure hunt was fun, but the real treasure was making friends."

"That's so cheesy," Elena said, grinning. "I love it."

They all laughed, and the sound echoed through the oak tree's branches, mixing with the sounds of their neighborhood - kids playing, dogs barking, and the ice cream truck playing its cheerful song in the distance.

Suki had learned something important: home wasn't just a place. It was the people you shared it with.

illustrations.description: The renovated treehouse glows with warm light as all four friends relax inside surrounded by colorful painted walls, cushions, and books. Suki draws in her sketchbook while the others work on various projects. Through the window opening, the peaceful neighborhood can be seen in the golden evening light, suggesting belonging and home.
illustrations.alt: The friends enjoying their renovated treehouse headquarters together

metadata.illustrationStyle: Warm, inviting adventure illustration style. Earthy tones, golden light. Detailed environments with hidden details kids can discover.

questions.question: Where did Suki find the treasure map?
questions.explanation: Suki found the old treasure map tucked between the boards in the treehouse in her backyard.

questions.question: What does F.T.F. stand for?
questions.explanation: The Henderson kids explained in their note that F.T.F. means 'Friends Through Fun.'

questions.question: Who are Suki's three new friends?
questions.explanation: Suki's three new friends are Elena (who likes science experiments), Marcus (who wears glasses and brought a compass), and Zara (who brought a magnifying glass).

questions.question: What was the first clue location according to the map?
questions.explanation: The first symbol looked like a swing set, which led them to the playground behind the school.

questions.question: What did they find in the final treasure box?
choices: Four friendship bracelets and a note
questions.explanation: The wooden box contained four colorful friendship bracelets and a note from the Henderson kids explaining the real treasure was friendship.

questions.question: Why was Suki worried about moving to the new house?
choices: She didn't like the yellow color
She was afraid she wouldn't make friends
questions.explanation: Suki was worried about starting at a new school and not knowing anyone, asking her mom 'What if nobody here likes me? What if I don't make any friends?'

questions.question: What do the friends plan to do at the end of the story?
choices: Create their own treasure hunt for future kids
questions.explanation: Elena suggests they should make their own treasure hunt for the next kids who move to the neighborhood, and they all think it's a great idea.

pages.title: Complete the Treasure Hunt
pages.instructions: Fill in the missing words from the story!
pages.text: Suki found an old _____ map in the treehouse. She showed it to her three new friends: Elena, _____, and Zara. Together they followed the clues around the _____. The first clue was at the playground, the second at the _____, and the third at Maple _____. The final riddle led them back to Suki's _____ where they found a wooden box with friendship _____ inside.

pages.title: Friendship Riddle
pages.instructions: Solve the riddle just like Suki and her friends did!
pages.text: The Henderson kids left this riddle: 'Where friends _____ when the day is done, look for the place where _____ runs.' The answer was the community _____ where kids gather after school and have fun together. Can you think of places in your neighborhood where friends gather and _____ fills the air?
